content:
# Exodus Chapter 17 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~43r-43v (PDF pp. 81-82); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 58-59 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_017_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_017_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_017_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_017_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT NOTE -- TWO-PART NARRATIVE: WATER FROM THE ROCK AND WAR WITH AMALEK:** The Leningrad Codex presents Exodus 17 in standard prose column format across two pages. A *petuchah* (open paragraph) marker appears before 17:1, marking the transition from the manna narrative (ch. 16) to the water crisis at Rephidim. A *setumah* (closed paragraph) before v. 8 separates the water-from-the-rock episode from the Amalek battle -- a shift from internal crisis to external threat. A *petuchah* before v. 14 marks the transition from battle narrative to divine speech and memorial. These paragraph divisions align the chapter into three units: water crisis (vv. 1-7), battle (vv. 8-13), and memorial (vv. 14-16). The chapter divides into two distinct narratives -- the water crisis at Massah and Meribah (vv. 1-7) and the battle with Amalek (vv. 8-16) -- that together answer the question posed in v. 7: "Is YHWH in our midst or not?" The first narrative follows the established wilderness complaint pattern but escalates to a legal register: the people *riv* (quarrel, contend at law) with Moses and *nassah* (test, put on trial) YHWH. The second narrative introduces Joshua for the first time and presents victory as dependent not on military prowess but on Moses' raised hands holding the staff of God. The chapter concludes with an altar-name -- YHWH Nissi ("YHWH is my banner") -- and a cryptic oath of perpetual war against Amalek.

---

## Water from the Rock at Massah and Meribah (vv. 1-7)

**17:1** And all the congregation of the sons of Israel journeyed from the wilderness of Sin, by their stages, according to the mouth of YHWH, and they encamped at Rephidim. And there was no water for the people to drink. [fn1]

**17:2** And the people quarreled with Moses, and they said, "Give us water that we may drink!" And Moses said to them, "Why do you quarrel with me? Why do you test YHWH?" [fn2]

**17:3** And the people thirsted there for water, and the people grumbled against Moses, and said, "Why is this -- you brought us up from Egypt, to kill me and my sons and my livestock with thirst?" [fn3]

**17:4** And Moses cried out to YHWH, saying, "What shall I do with this people? A little more and they will stone me!" [fn4]

**17:5** And YHWH said to Moses, "Pass over before the people, and take with you some of the elders of Israel. And your staff with which you struck the Nile -- take in your hand and go. [fn5]

**17:6** Behold, I am standing before you there, upon the rock at Horeb. And you shall strike the rock, and water shall come out from it, and the people shall drink." And Moses did so in the sight of the elders of Israel. [fn6]

**17:7** And he called the name of the place Massah and Meribah, because of the quarreling of the sons of Israel, and because of their testing YHWH, saying, "Is YHWH in our midst, or not?" [fn7]

---

## The Battle with Amalek (vv. 8-13)

**17:8** And Amalek came and fought with Israel at Rephidim. [fn8]

**17:9** And Moses said to Joshua, "Choose for us men, and go out, fight against Amalek. Tomorrow I will station myself on the top of the hill, with the staff of God in my hand." [fn9]

**17:10** And Joshua did as Moses told him, to fight against Amalek. And Moses, Aaron, and Hur went up to the top of the hill. [fn10]

**17:11** And it came to pass, when Moses raised his hand, Israel prevailed; and when he let his hand rest, Amalek prevailed. [fn11]

**17:12** And the hands of Moses were heavy. And they took a stone and placed it under him, and he sat upon it. And Aaron and Hur supported his hands -- one on this side and one on that side. And his hands were steady until the going down of the sun. [fn12]

**17:13** And Joshua weakened Amalek and his people with the edge of the sword. [fn13]

---

## YHWH Nissi -- "YHWH Is My Banner" (vv. 14-16)

**17:14** And YHWH said to Moses, "Write this as a memorial in the book, and set it in the ears of Joshua, that I will surely blot out the remembrance of Amalek from under the heavens." [fn14]

**17:15** And Moses built an altar, and he called its name "YHWH Nissi" -- "YHWH Is My Banner." [fn15]

**17:16** And he said, "For a hand is upon the throne of Yah -- war for YHWH against Amalek from generation to generation." [fn16]

---

> **[fn1] v.1 -- *Vayyis'u...lemasse'eyhem al-pi YHWH*: "journeyed...by their stages, according to the mouth of YHWH" -- the divinely directed route to a waterless place** -- Hebrew: *vayyis'u kol-adat benei-Yisra'el mi-midbar-Sin le-mas'eihem al-pi YHWH vayyakhanu bi-Rfidim ve-ein mayim lishtot ha-am*. The noun *massa'* (plural *masse'ot*, with suffix *mas'eihem*, "their stages/journeyings," from *nasa'*, "to pull up tent pegs, to set out") indicates a multi-stage journey; Numbers 33:12-14 fills in intermediate stations (Dophkah, Alush). The phrase *al-pi YHWH* ("upon the mouth of YHWH" = "at YHWH's command") indicates that the people arrived at a waterless place not by accident but by divine direction -- every stage of the journey was commanded. The name *Rfidim* (Rephidim) is of uncertain etymology; some connect it to *rafad* ("to spread out, support"), yielding "resting places" -- ironic given the absence of water. The clause *ve-ein mayim lishtot ha-am* ("and there was no water for the people to drink") states the crisis with stark simplicity. EMET renders "by their journeyings [le-mas'eihem], upon the mouth of [al-pi] YHWH" with extensive transliteration. LOGOS renders "by their stages, according to the mouth of the LORD." PNEUMA renders "traveling in stages as YHWH directed." The consensus renders "by their stages, according to the mouth of YHWH" -- following LOGOS for the formal English while preserving the transliterated divine name. "Stages" (LOGOS) rather than "journeyings" (EMET) for *mas'eihem* captures the technical itinerary sense. PNEUMA's "as YHWH directed" is accurate but loses the characteristic Hebrew idiom "the mouth of YHWH." LXX: *kata parembolas autōn* ("according to their encampments") for *le-mas'eihem*; *dia rhēmatos kuriou* ("by the word of the Lord") for *al-pi YHWH*.

> **[fn2] v.2 -- *Vayyarev...tenassun*: "quarreled...test" -- the two verbs that become place names** -- Hebrew: *vayyarev ha-am im-Mosheh vayyomeru tenu-lanu mayim ve-nishteh vayyomer lahem Mosheh mah-terivun immadi mah-tenassun et-YHWH*. Two critical verbs define this verse and the entire episode. The verb *vayyarev* (Qal of *riv*, "to quarrel, contend, bring a lawsuit") introduces legal/forensic language -- this is not mere grumbling (*lun*, as in 15:24 and 16:2) but a formal dispute, a *riv*. The people are bringing charges against Moses. This root generates the place name *Merivah* ("Quarreling/Contention") in v. 7. The verb *tenassun* (Piel of *nasah*, "to test, to put to the proof," with paragogic *nun*) frames the people's demand as a theological act -- they are putting YHWH on trial, demanding that He prove His presence by providing water. This root generates the place name *Massah* ("Testing") in v. 7. Moses' double question -- *mah-terivun...mah-tenassun* -- distinguishes two dimensions: the quarrel is directed at Moses (*immadi*, "with me"), but the testing is directed at YHWH (*et-YHWH*). To contend with YHWH's appointed leader is to put YHWH Himself on trial. EMET renders "quarreled [vayarev]" and "test [tenassun]" with transliterated bracketing. LOGOS renders "quarreled" and "test." PNEUMA renders "quarreled" and "putting YHWH to the test." All three agree on "quarreled" for *vayyarev*. The consensus renders "quarreled" and "test" -- all three translators converge on these renderings. "Quarreled" (rather than the LXX's stronger "reviled") preserves the legal/forensic dimension of *riv*. "Test" (rather than "tempt" or "try") captures the hostile probing quality of *nasah*. LXX: *eloidoreito* ("reviled, reproached") for *vayyarev* -- a stronger term that shifts from legal contention to verbal abuse; *peirazete* ("you test") for *tenassun*.

> **[fn3] v.3 -- *Lehamit oti ve-et-banai ve-et-miqnai batsama*: "to kill me and my sons and my livestock with thirst" -- the singular voice of the collective** -- Hebrew: *vayyitsma sham ha-am la-mayim vayyallen ha-am al-Mosheh vayyomer lammah zeh he-elitanu mi-Mitsrayim le-hamit oti ve-et-banai ve-et-miqnai ba-tsama*. The people's speech shifts from plural (*ha-am*, "the people") to singular: *oti* ("me"), *banai* ("my sons"), *miqnai* ("my livestock"). This rhetorical shift makes the complaint intensely personal -- each family feels the threat of death individually. The verb *vayyallen* (Hiphil of *lun*, "to murmur, grumble") is the same complaint-verb from 15:24 and 16:2, now joined to the more aggressive *riv* of v. 2 -- the pattern escalates from grumbling to quarreling. The noun *miqneh* ("livestock, cattle," from *qanah*, "to acquire") extends the crisis beyond human life; the animals are dying too. EMET renders "grumbled [vayallen]" and preserves the singular. LOGOS renders "murmured" and preserves the singular. PNEUMA renders "complained" and shifts to "us and our children and our livestock" (plural). The consensus renders "grumbled" and preserves the singular voicing: "to kill me and my sons and my livestock." "Grumbled" maintains consistency with the rendering of *lun* established in EXO 15:24 and 16:2. The singular "me and my sons and my livestock" follows the MT against the LXX's plural harmonization; it preserves the Hebrew's rhetorically powerful singular-within-the-collective. PNEUMA's shift to plural ("us and our children") follows the LXX but the consensus follows the MT. LXX: *hēmas kai ta tekna hēmōn kai ta ktēnē* ("us and our children and our cattle") -- plural harmonization, losing the Hebrew's singular voicing.

> **[fn4] v.4 -- *Od me'at u-seqaluni*: "A little more and they will stone me" -- the threat of mob violence** -- Hebrew: *vayyits'aq Mosheh el-YHWH lemor mah e'eseh la-am ha-zeh od me'at u-seqaluni*. The verb *vayyits'aq* (from *tsa'aq*, "to cry out") is the same root used of Israel's outcry under Egyptian oppression (2:23; 3:7, 9) -- now Moses himself cries out, not against Pharaoh but under pressure from the people he liberated. The phrase *od me'at u-seqaluni* ("a little more and they will stone me") reveals that the situation has reached the threshold of lethal mob violence. Stoning (*saqal*) is the communal form of execution in ancient Israel (cf. Lev. 20:2; Num. 15:35-36). All three translators agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS for English phrasing: "What shall I do with this people?" -- rendering *la-am ha-zeh* as "with" rather than EMET's literal "for." PNEUMA's "What am I supposed to do with these people?" is too colloquial for the consensus register. LXX: *katalithobolēsousin me* ("they will stone me to death") -- the compound verb intensifies the threat.

> **[fn5] v.5 -- *U-mattkha asher hikkita bo et-ha-Ye'or*: "your staff with which you struck the Nile" -- the plague-staff becomes the provision-staff** -- Hebrew: *vayyomer YHWH el-Mosheh avor lifnei ha-am ve-qakh ittkha mi-ziqnei Yisra'el u-mattkha asher hikkita bo et-ha-Ye'or qakh be-yadkha ve-halakhta*. YHWH commands Moses to take three things: his own leadership position (*avor lifnei ha-am*, "pass over before the people"), some of the elders as witnesses (*mi-ziqnei Yisra'el*), and the staff (*mattkha*). The staff is specifically identified as the one that struck the Nile and turned it to blood (7:17, 20) -- the instrument of judgment becomes the instrument of provision. The same staff that made water undrinkable in Egypt will now draw drinkable water from rock. The elders serve as witnesses (*le-einei*, "before the eyes of," v. 6) to a public miracle. EMET renders "your matteh [staff] which you struck the Ye'or [Nile] with it." LOGOS renders "your staff with which you struck the River." PNEUMA renders "the staff with which you struck the Nile." The consensus renders "your staff with which you struck the Nile" -- following LOGOS/PNEUMA for smooth English, using "Nile" (PNEUMA) rather than the technical "River" (LOGOS) or transliterated "Ye'or" (EMET) for clarity. "Nile" is the standard English equivalent of *ha-Ye'or* in the Exodus context. LXX: *epataxas ton potamon* ("you struck the river").

> **[fn6] v.6 -- *Hineni omed lefanekha sham al-ha-tsur be-Chorev*: "Behold, I am standing before you there, upon the rock at Horeb" -- YHWH upon the rock** -- Hebrew: *hineni omed lefanekha sham al-ha-tsur be-Chorev ve-hikkita va-tsur ve-yatse'u mimmennu mayim ve-shatah ha-am vayyaas ken Mosheh le-einei ziqnei Yisra'el*. This is one of the most theologically extraordinary statements in Exodus. YHWH declares: *hineni omed lefanekha sham al-ha-tsur* -- "behold, I am standing before you there, upon the rock." The word *hineni* ("behold me, here I am") marks solemn divine self-presentation. YHWH positions Himself *al-ha-tsur* ("upon the rock") -- the rock at Horeb is the place of divine presence. When Moses strikes the rock, he strikes the place where YHWH stands, and water flows from beneath God's feet. The noun *tsur* ("rock, cliff, crag") is one of the primary divine metaphors in the Hebrew Bible: YHWH is "the Rock" (*ha-Tsur*, Deut. 32:4, 15, 18, 30, 31; 2 Sam. 22:2-3; Ps. 18:2). The rock is both literal geology and theological sign. The location *Chorev* (Horeb) is the mountain of God, another name for Sinai (cf. 3:1), where Moses first encountered the burning bush. The water miracle occurs at the very mountain where YHWH will give the Torah. The command *ve-hikkita va-tsur* ("and you shall strike the rock") uses the same Hiphil of *nakah* used for striking the Nile -- the same verb, the same staff, opposite outcomes: blood in Egypt, water in the wilderness. EMET renders "Behold me [hineni] standing [omed] before you [lefanekha] there [sham] upon [al] the rock [ha-tsur] in Khorev [Horeb]." LOGOS renders "Behold, I am standing before you there, upon the rock at Horeb." PNEUMA renders "I will be standing there before you on the rock at Horeb." The consensus renders "Behold, I am standing before you there, upon the rock at Horeb" -- following LOGOS for formal English while preserving the participial construction (*omed*, present participle). EMET's heavy bracketing is reduced for readability. PNEUMA's "I will be standing" shifts to future tense; the Hebrew is a present participle with *hineni* -- YHWH declares present, not future, positioning. The word order "before you there" (LOGOS) preserves the Hebrew sequence *lefanekha sham*. LXX: *hode egō hestēka pro tou se ekei epi tēs petras en Chōrēb* ("behold, I stand before you there upon the rock in Choreb"). The LXX adds *ho laos mou* ("my people") for *ha-am* ("the people") -- making explicit YHWH's possessive claim; the consensus follows the MT without the addition. The LXX also reads "before the sons of Israel" rather than "before the eyes of the elders of Israel" -- a broadening of the witness group; the consensus follows the MT.

> **[fn7] v.7 -- *Ha-yesh YHWH be-qirbenu im-ayin*: "Is YHWH in our midst, or not?" -- the question beneath all questions** -- Hebrew: *vayyiqra shem ha-maqom Massah u-Merivah al-riv benei Yisra'el ve-al nassotam et-YHWH lemor ha-yesh YHWH be-qirbenu im-ayin*. The dual naming is etiological: *Massah* ("Testing," from *nasah*, "to test") and *Merivah* ("Quarreling," from *riv*, "to quarrel, contend at law") -- each name derives from a key verb in the preceding narrative. The etiological formula *al-riv...ve-al nassotam* explains the naming with precise causal markers. These names become permanent markers in Israel's memory (cf. Deut. 6:16; 9:22; 33:8; Ps. 95:8; 106:32). The climactic question *ha-yesh YHWH be-qirbenu im-ayin* is the theological crux of the entire chapter. The interrogative *ha-yesh* ("is there?") with *be-qirbenu* ("in our midst," from *qerev*, "midst, inner part") asks not "Is God real?" but "Is God *here*, with us, in the center of our community?" The alternative *im-ayin* ("or not," literally "or there-is-not") poses the starkest theological disjunction: presence or absence, being or nothingness. This question echoes through the Torah and the Prophets as the fundamental wilderness question; Psalm 95:7-11 warns later generations never to repeat it. All three translators render the place names without translation, preserving the Hebrew toponyms. All three render the question essentially the same way. The consensus renders "Is YHWH in our midst, or not?" -- following LOGOS/EMET for the formal phrasing. LXX: *Peirasmos kai Loidorēsis* ("Testing and Reviling") -- translating both names, making the etymology transparent to Greek readers but losing the Hebrew toponyms. The LXX renders *riv* as "reviling" (*loidoria*) rather than "quarreling," again intensifying beyond the Hebrew.

> **[fn8] v.8 -- *Vayyavo Amaleq vayyillakhem im-Yisra'el*: "Amalek came and fought with Israel" -- the unprovoked attack** -- Hebrew: *vayyavo Amaleq vayyillakhem im-Yisra'el bi-Rfidim*. Amalek (*Amaleq*) is identified in Genesis 36:12 as a grandson of Esau through Eliphaz and his concubine Timna -- making the Amalekites distant kin of Israel. Deuteronomy 25:17-18 adds a crucial detail: Amalek attacked the rear of the column -- the weak, the weary, the stragglers -- "and he did not fear God." The attack comes without provocation, without warning, at a moment of maximum Israelite vulnerability. The verb *vayyillakhem* (Niphal of *lakham*, "to fight, to wage war") indicates formal military engagement. All three translators agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS for "fought." EMET renders "he fought [vayillakhem]" with transliteration; PNEUMA renders "attacked" -- "fought" is preferred as a direct rendering of *vayyillakhem*; PNEUMA's "attacked" is interpretive (implying aggression), though contextually accurate. LXX: *epolemei* ("was waging war") -- imperfect tense, suggesting ongoing battle rather than a single engagement.

> **[fn9] v.9 -- *Yehoshua*: "Joshua" -- the first appearance, and *matteh ha-Elohim*: "the staff of God"** -- Hebrew: *vayyomer Mosheh el-Yehoshua bekhar-lanu anashim ve-tse hillakhem ba-Amaleq makhar anokhi nitsav al-rosh ha-giv'ah u-matteh ha-Elohim be-yadi*. This is the first appearance of Joshua (*Yehoshua*, "YHWH saves," from the divine name + *yasha'*, "to save, deliver") in the biblical text. He appears without introduction, without genealogy, without patronymic -- as though the reader already knows him. He is later identified as *bin-Nun* ("son of Nun," 33:11; Num. 11:28) and as Moses' *meshareth* ("attendant, minister"). His abrupt entrance given a military command foreshadows his future role as Israel's war-leader. The verb *bekhar* (Qal imperative of *bakhar*, "to choose, to select") implies discernment -- not all men, but chosen men. This is the first military mobilization in Israel's history as a free people. Moses' own position is intercessory, not martial: he stands on the hilltop (*al-rosh ha-giv'ah*, "on the top of the hill") with the *matteh ha-Elohim* ("staff of God") -- the staff now explicitly named as a divine instrument, not merely Moses' tool. The participle *nitsav* (Niphal of *yatsav*, "to station oneself") indicates a deliberate, fixed posture. EMET renders "Yehoshua" (transliterated). LOGOS renders "Joshua." PNEUMA renders "Joshua." The consensus renders "Joshua" -- the standard English form, following LOGOS/PNEUMA. The Hebrew name *Yehoshua* ("YHWH saves") -- the same name rendered in Greek as *Iēsous*, from which "Jesus" derives -- is noted but not transliterated in the running text. For "Choose for us men," all three translators agree on substance. The consensus follows LOGOS for the phrasing: "Choose for us men, and go out, fight against Amalek." PNEUMA's "Choose men for us" inverts the Hebrew word order (*bekhar-lanu anashim*); EMET/LOGOS preserve it. LXX adds *dunatos* ("mighty") to describe the men -- not in the Hebrew; the consensus follows the MT.

> **[fn10] v.10 -- *Chur*: "Hur" -- the silent supporter** -- Hebrew: *vayyaas Yehoshua ka-asher amar-lo Mosheh le-hillakhem ba-Amaleq u-Mosheh Aharon ve-Chur alu rosh ha-giv'ah*. Like Joshua, Hur (*Chur*) appears without introduction. He is mentioned again at Exodus 24:14, where Moses leaves him and Aaron in charge. According to 1 Chronicles 2:19-20, Hur was the son of Caleb and Ephrath, and the grandfather of Bezalel, the chief craftsman of the Tabernacle. Josephus (Antiquities 3.2.4) identifies Hur as Miriam's husband, though this has no direct biblical support. Three figures ascend the hill: Moses the intercessor with the staff, Aaron and Hur as supporters. The battle unfolds on two planes simultaneously -- in the valley (Joshua and the warriors) and on the hilltop (Moses, Aaron, and Hur). All three translators agree on substance. The consensus renders "Hur" -- following LOGOS/PNEUMA. EMET renders "Khur" (closer to the Hebrew *Chur*); "Hur" is the standard English form. LXX: *Ōr* (Or) for *Chur*.

> **[fn11] v.11 -- *Yarim...yanikah*: "raised...let rest" -- the raised hands and the battle's outcome** -- Hebrew: *ve-hayah ka-asher yarim Mosheh yado ve-gavar Yisra'el ve-kha-asher yanikah yado ve-gavar Amaleq*. The pattern is direct and causal: when Moses *yarim* (Hiphil of *rum*, "to raise, lift up") his hand, Israel *gavar* ("prevailed, was strong"); when he *yanikah* (Hiphil of *nuakh*, "to rest, set down") his hand, Amalek *gavar*. The same verb (*gavar*) is used for both sides -- the only variable is Moses' hand. The MT reads *yado* ("his hand," singular), though the LXX reads the plural *cheiras* ("hands"), harmonizing with v. 12 where the MT itself uses the plural *yadav* ("his hands"). The Mishnah (Rosh Hashanah 3:8) interprets: "Could the hands of Moses win the war or lose the war? Rather, when Israel looked upward and directed their hearts to their Father in heaven, they prevailed." The raised hands are not magic but a visible sign of intercessory dependence on YHWH. EMET renders "raised [yarim]...rested [yanikah]" with transliteration. LOGOS renders "raised...let down." PNEUMA renders "raised...lowered." The consensus renders "raised...let his hand rest" -- following EMET for "rest" which preserves the Hebrew *yanikah* (from *nuakh*, "to rest") rather than LOGOS's "let down" or PNEUMA's "lowered." The verb *nuakh* specifically means "to rest, to set at rest" -- not merely "to lower" -- and the consensus preserves this nuance. The singular "his hand" follows the MT against the LXX's plural harmonization. LXX: *tas cheiras* ("the hands," plural) throughout, harmonizing with v. 12.

> **[fn12] v.12 -- *Emunah*: "steady" -- the faithfulness of Moses' hands** -- Hebrew: *vi-dei Mosheh kevedim vayyiqkhu even vayyasimu takhtav vayyeshev aleha ve-Aharon ve-Chur tamkhu ve-yadav mi-zeh ekhad u-mi-zeh ekhad vayehi yadav emunah ad-bo ha-shemesh*. This verse is dense with theological significance. The word *kevedim* ("heavy," from *kaved*, "to be heavy") shares its root with *kavod* ("glory, weight") -- Moses' hands are heavy with the weight of intercession. The verb *tamkhu* (from *tamakh*, "to support, uphold, sustain") describes what Aaron and Hur do -- the same verb used in Psalm 63:9 ("Your right hand upholds me") and Isaiah 42:1 ("I uphold him"). Intercession is not solitary; it requires a community of support. The word *emunah* ("steadiness, faithfulness, firmness," from *aman*, "to be firm, to be faithful") is one of the most theologically loaded terms in the Hebrew Bible. Its cognate verb *he'emin* ("to believe, to trust") is the verb of Abraham's faith in Genesis 15:6. Here applied to Moses' hands -- *vayehi yadav emunah* ("and his hands were *emunah*") -- the word creates a semantic bridge between physical steadiness and theological faithfulness. The hands are literally "faithfulness" -- steady, firm, unwavering. This is the same word used in Habakkuk 2:4 (*tsaddiq be-emunato yikhyeh*, "the righteous shall live by his faithfulness"). The phrase *ad-bo ha-shemesh* ("until the going down of the sun") indicates an all-day battle. EMET renders "steadfast [emunah]" with transliteration. LOGOS renders "steady." PNEUMA renders "steady." The consensus renders "steady" -- following LOGOS/PNEUMA, with full acknowledgment in this note that the Hebrew *emunah* carries the deeper resonance of "faithfulness, firmness" beyond mere physical stability. "Steady" is chosen for the running text because it accurately describes the physical state of the hands without importing an anachronistic spiritual reading into the translation itself. The theological depth is noted rather than translated. "Steadfast" (EMET) is a viable alternative but slightly overreads the immediate physical context. LXX: *estērigmenai* ("supported, established") -- conveying firmness but losing the faith-connotation entirely.

> **[fn13] v.13 -- *Vayyakhalosh*: "weakened" -- the hapax legomenon** -- Hebrew: *vayyakhalosh Yehoshua et-Amaleq ve-et-ammo lefi-kharev*. The verb *vayyakhalosh* (from *khalash*, "to be weak, to weaken, to prostrate") is a hapax legomenon -- it occurs only here in the entire Hebrew Bible. The choice of *khalash* rather than the more common *nakah* ("to strike") or *harag* ("to kill") is notable: Joshua weakened or disabled Amalek, he did not annihilate him. The victory is decisive but not total -- Amalek survives to fight again (cf. 1 Sam. 15; 30). The phrase *lefi-kharev* ("by the mouth of the sword," literally "to the mouth of the sword") is a standard Hebrew idiom for death in battle -- the sword has a "mouth" that devours. EMET renders "weakened [vayakhalosh]." LOGOS renders "weakened." PNEUMA renders "overwhelmed." The consensus renders "weakened" -- following EMET/LOGOS. This is the most faithful rendering of the hapax *khalash*, which denotes diminishment of strength rather than total destruction. PNEUMA's "overwhelmed" suggests a more complete victory than the Hebrew warrants; the text's choice of a "weakening" verb rather than a "destroying" verb is theologically deliberate -- Amalek is weakened but not exterminated, necessitating the perpetual-war declaration in v. 16. LXX: *etrepsen* ("he routed") -- suggesting the enemy was put to flight, another interpretive variant.

> **[fn14] v.14 -- *Ketov zot zikkaron ba-sefer*: "Write this as a memorial in the book" -- writing, memory, and the oath of obliteration** -- Hebrew: *vayyomer YHWH el-Mosheh ketov zot zikkaron ba-sefer ve-sim be-oznei Yehoshua ki-makhoh emkheh et-zekher Amaleq mi-takhat ha-shamayim*. This verse contains one of the earliest explicit divine commands to write in the Torah. The noun *zikkaron* ("memorial, remembrance," from *zakhar*, "to remember") indicates that the written record serves a mnemonic function. The definite article *ba-sefer* ("in *the* book/scroll") implies a specific, known document -- possibly the emerging Torah itself or a pre-existing record (cf. Num. 21:14, "the Book of the Wars of YHWH"). The command *ve-sim be-oznei Yehoshua* ("and set it in the ears of Joshua") bridges the written and the oral: it must be both inscribed and recited. The emphatic construction *makhoh emkheh* (infinitive absolute + imperfect of *makhah*, "to blot out, obliterate") expresses absolute certainty: "I will surely blot out." The irony is devastating: the memorial (*zikkaron*) preserves the promise to erase the remembrance (*zekher*) of Amalek. Writing preserves what blotting-out destroys -- and both are commanded in the same breath. The message is directed specifically to Joshua because he will carry the mandate forward. EMET renders "a memorial [zikkaron] in the sefer [scroll/book]" with transliteration. LOGOS renders "a memorial in the book." PNEUMA renders "a memorial in a document." The consensus renders "a memorial in the book" -- following LOGOS. "Book" (LOGOS) is preferred over "scroll/book" (EMET) or "document" (PNEUMA) because the Hebrew *sefer* with the definite article implies a known, authoritative written collection. For *ve-sim be-oznei Yehoshua*, the consensus renders "set it in the ears of Joshua" -- following LOGOS for the literal Hebrew idiom. PNEUMA's "recite it aloud to Joshua" is a valid dynamic rendering but loses the vivid image. LXX: *mnēmosynon eis biblion* ("a memorial into a book"); *paradōs eis ōta Iēsou* ("deliver into the ears of Joshua"), using *paradidōmi* ("to hand over") for *sim* ("to set").

> **[fn15] v.15 -- *YHWH Nissi*: "YHWH Is My Banner" -- the altar-name as theological declaration** -- Hebrew: *vayyiven Mosheh mizbe'akh vayyiqra shemo YHWH Nissi*. Moses builds an altar (*mizbe'akh*, from *zavakh*, "to slaughter, sacrifice") -- but the altar's significance lies not in sacrifice but in its name. The name *YHWH Nissi* ("YHWH is my banner") derives from *nes* ("banner, standard, signal, ensign") -- the raised standard around which troops rally in battle, the visible sign that gives direction and courage (cf. Isa. 5:26; 11:10, 12). By naming the altar *YHWH Nissi*, Moses declares that YHWH Himself is the rallying point of Israel's warfare. The connection to the raised hands is implicit: Moses' uplifted hands holding the staff of God functioned as the battle standard. The altar transforms the site of battle into a site of worship; the victory is attributed entirely to YHWH. All three translators agree on "YHWH Nissi" and "YHWH Is My Banner." The consensus preserves both the Hebrew name (transliterated and italicized) and its English translation. LXX: *Kurios mou kataphugē* ("the Lord is my refuge") -- a significant interpretive divergence, deriving *nissi* from *nus* ("to flee for refuge") rather than *nes* ("banner"). The consensus follows the MT's "banner" reading, which fits the military context of the chapter and the motif of Moses' raised hands as a visible standard.

> **[fn16] v.16 -- *Ki-yad al-kes Yah*: "a hand is upon the throne of Yah" -- the defective throne and the abbreviated Name** -- Hebrew: *vayyomer ki-yad al-kes Yah milkhamah la-YHWH ba-Amaleq mi-dor dor*. This is the most cryptic verse in the chapter and one of the most obscure in the Pentateuch. The word *kes* is an unusual, truncated form of *kisse'* ("throne") -- the defective spelling has no parallel elsewhere in the Hebrew Bible. The name *Yah* is the shortened form of YHWH, appearing primarily in poetic contexts (as in *Hallelu-Yah*, "praise Yah!"). The combination of both truncated forms -- *kes* for *kisse'* and *Yah* for *YHWH* -- has generated extensive rabbinic commentary. The Midrash (Tanchuma, Ki Tetse 11; cf. Rashi) interprets: "God swore that neither His Name nor His throne will be complete until the name of Amalek is utterly blotted out" -- both the throne and the divine name remain *defective* as long as Amalek exists, signaling cosmic incompleteness. The phrase *ki-yad al-kes Yah* has been read multiple ways: (a) God's hand is raised upon His own throne as an oath-taking gesture -- He swears perpetual war; (b) a hand (Amalek's) is raised *against* God's throne -- the attack on Israel is an assault on divine sovereignty; (c) the hand (Moses' hand from vv. 11-12) is memorialized as "upon the throne of Yah." The clause *milkhamah la-YHWH ba-Amaleq mi-dor dor* ("war for YHWH against Amalek from generation to generation") declares perpetual warfare -- the conflict is multigenerational, not a single battle. The war is *la-YHWH* -- it belongs to YHWH, it is waged on YHWH's behalf. The consensus preserves the defective forms. The rendering uses "the throne of Yah" rather than normalizing to "the throne of YHWH" or "the throne of the LORD" -- the abbreviated forms *kes* and *Yah* are preserved because the defective spelling is theologically significant and cannot be normalized without losing meaning. EMET renders "the throne [kes] of Yah [YHWH]" with parenthetical expansion. LOGOS renders "the throne of YAH" (capitalized). PNEUMA renders "the throne of YH" (consonantal). The consensus renders "the throne of Yah" -- using the standard English transliteration *Yah* (as in "Hallelujah") without the bracketed expansion. The short form is preserved as it appears in the Hebrew. The word "throne" renders *kes* (the defective form); a note in the disagreement log addresses whether to normalize *kes* to *kisse'*. LXX: *hoti en cheiri kryphaiai polemei kyrios epi Amalēk* ("because with a hidden hand the Lord wages war against Amalek") -- a radically different reading. The LXX apparently derives *kes* from *kasah* ("to cover, conceal") rather than *kisse'* ("throne"), yielding "hidden" instead of "throne." The consensus follows the MT.

---
